title: At the Bakery
summary: A customer visits a small bakery and orders bread and pastries. She chats with the friendly baker while choosing what to buy.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Good morning! Something smells amazing in here.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Good morning! Everything is fresh from the oven this morning. What can I get for you?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: I'd like a loaf of white bread, please. Do you have any left?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Yes, we have plenty. Would you like the small loaf or the large one?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: The large one, please. And I can see some croissants over there. Are they butter croissants?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: They are! We bake them fresh every morning. They're still warm.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Oh, wonderful! I'll take two, please. What are those little round cakes on the top shelf?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Those are blueberry muffins. They're very popular. Would you like to try one?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Yes, I'll have one muffin too, please. How much is everything?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: That's one large loaf, two croissants, and one muffin. That comes to seven dollars and fifty cents.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Here you are. Thank you so much. Everything looks delicious!

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Thank you! Enjoy your breakfast. Have a lovely day!

annotations.meaningVi: mới ra lò, vừa nướng xong
annotations.exampleEn: The bread is fresh from the oven — you can smell it from the street.
We bought a loaf of bread at the supermarket.
Don't worry — there's plenty of food for everyone.
She had a croissant and coffee for breakfast.
A butter croissant is flaky and rich in flavour.
She baked blueberry muffins for the school bake sale.
The most expensive wines are on the top shelf.
Chocolate cake is very popular with children.
Your total comes to fifteen dollars.

annotations.meaningVi: đây ạ (khi đưa vật gì cho người khác)
annotations.exampleEn: 'Here you are,' she said, handing him the book.
What a wonderful idea — let's do it!

grammarNotes.title: Polite Requests with 'I'd like'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I'd like' (= I would like) là cách lịch sự để đặt hàng hoặc yêu cầu điều gì. Lịch sự hơn 'I want'. Thêm 'please' vào cuối câu để tăng sự lịch sự. Ví dụ: 'I'd like a loaf of bread, please.' (Cho tôi một ổ bánh mì, làm ơn.)
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I'd like' is a polite way to order or request something. It is more formal and courteous than 'I want'. Adding 'please' at the end makes it even more polite. Example: 'I'd like two croissants, please.'

grammarNotes.title: Questions with 'Are they...?' / 'Do you have...?'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để hỏi về thông tin sản phẩm, chúng ta dùng 'Do you have...?' (Bạn có... không?) hoặc 'Are they...?' (Chúng có phải là... không?). Ví dụ: 'Do you have any left?' (Bạn còn hàng không?) và 'Are they butter croissants?' (Chúng có phải là bánh croissant bơ không?)
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To ask about products or items, we use 'Do you have...?' and 'Are they...?'. These are yes/no questions. 'Do you have any left?' checks if something is available. 'Are they butter croissants?' confirms what something is.

translationVi:
Khách hàng: Chào buổi sáng! Trong này thơm quá nhỉ.
Bánh mì: Chào buổi sáng! Mọi thứ đều mới ra lò sáng nay. Tôi có thể lấy gì cho bạn?
Khách hàng: Tôi muốn một ổ bánh mì trắng, làm ơn. Bạn còn không?
Bánh mì: Vâng, còn nhiều lắm ạ. Bạn muốn ổ nhỏ hay ổ lớn?
Khách hàng: Ổ lớn, làm ơn. Và tôi thấy có mấy cái bánh sừng bò ở đằng kia. Chúng có phải là bánh sừng bò bơ không?
Bánh mì: Đúng vậy! Chúng tôi nướng tươi mỗi sáng. Bánh vẫn còn ấm đó ạ.
Khách hàng: Ồ, tuyệt quá! Cho tôi hai cái, làm ơn. Những cái bánh tròn nhỏ trên kệ trên cùng kia là gì vậy?
Bánh mì: Đó là bánh muffin việt quất ạ. Rất được ưa chuộng đó. Bạn có muốn thử một cái không?
Khách hàng: Vâng, cho tôi một cái muffin nữa, làm ơn. Tất cả hết bao nhiêu tiền?
Bánh mì: Một ổ bánh mì lớn, hai cái croissant, và một cái muffin. Tổng cộng là bảy đô la năm mươi xu.
Khách hàng: Đây ạ. Cảm ơn rất nhiều. Mọi thứ trông ngon tuyệt!
Bánh mì: Cảm ơn bạn! Chúc bạn ăn sáng ngon miệng. Chúc một ngày tuyệt vời!

questions.prompt: What time of day does this conversation take place?
questions.explanation: The conversation begins with 'Good morning!' and the baker says everything is 'fresh from the oven this morning.'
questions.hint: Listen to the greeting they use.

questions.prompt: What size of bread loaf does the customer choose?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'The large one, please.'
questions.hint: The baker offered two options — which did the customer pick?

questions.prompt: What type of croissants does the bakery sell?
questions.explanation: The customer asks 'Are they butter croissants?' and the baker confirms 'They are!'
questions.hint: What question did the customer ask about the croissants?

questions.prompt: How many croissants does the customer buy?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'I'll take two, please.'

questions.prompt: Where are the muffins displayed in the bakery?
questions.explanation: The customer asks 'What are those little round cakes on the top shelf?'
questions.hint: Where did the customer notice the muffins?

questions.explanation: The baker says 'Those are blueberry muffins.'
questions.hint: What fruit did the baker mention?

questions.prompt: How does the baker describe the muffins?
questions.hint: What word did the baker use to describe how much people like the muffins?

questions.prompt: How much does the customer pay in total?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'That comes to seven dollars and fifty cents.'
questions.hint: Listen for the total amount the baker calculates.

questions.prompt: How does the baker describe the croissants?
What does the baker wish the customer at the end?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'Have a lovely day!'
questions.hint: What was the baker's final farewell?

questions.prompt: What does the customer say she notices when she first walks in?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'Something smells amazing in here.'
questions.hint: What sense did the customer use first?

cloze.template:
Customer: Good morning! Something smells {{b1}} in here.
Baker: Good morning! Everything is fresh from the {{b2}} this morning. What can I get for you?
Customer: I'd like a {{b3}} of white bread, please. Do you have any left?
Baker: Yes, we have {{b4}}. Would you like the small loaf or the large one?
Customer: The large one, please. And I can see some croissants over there. Are they {{b5}} croissants?
Baker: They are! We {{b6}} them fresh every morning. They're still warm.
Customer: Oh, wonderful! I'll take two, please. What are those little round cakes on the top {{b7}}?
Baker: Those are blueberry {{b8}}. They're very popular. Would you like to try one?
Customer: Yes, I'll have one muffin too, please. How much is everything?
Baker: That's one large loaf, two croissants, and one muffin. That {{b9}} to seven dollars and fifty cents.
Customer: Here you are. Thank you so much. Everything looks {{b10}}!
Baker: Thank you! Enjoy your breakfast. Have a lovely day!

blanks.explanation: The customer says 'Something smells amazing' — a positive way to describe a wonderful smell.
'Fresh from the oven' is a fixed phrase meaning baked very recently.
Bread is counted in 'loaves' (singular: loaf). A loaf is a whole unsliced bread.
'We have plenty' means there is a lot available — no need to worry about running out.
The customer confirms they are 'butter croissants' — made with a lot of butter.
'Bake' is the specific verb for cooking bread, cakes, and pastries in an oven.
'Top shelf' means the highest level of a shelving unit in the store.
The baker says 'Those are blueberry muffins' — small round baked goods.
'That comes to...' is a natural phrase used when telling someone a price total.
'Everything looks delicious' means the food looks very tasty and appealing.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The baker bakes everything fresh every morning. This probably means some food goes unsold at the end of the day. Do you think it is better to bake fresh every day even if food is wasted, or to bake less and risk running out? What would you do if you ran the bakery?
